title: US Doubles Bounty on El Mochomito to $10M — Total $20M Now Offered for Both Chapitos Brothers
detail: The U.S. government doubled the reward for Jesús Alfredo 'El Mochomito' Guzmán Salazar to $10 million on April 16, 2026, matching the reward previously reiterated for his brother Iván Archivaldo on April 14. The combined $20 million bounty targets the two remaining at-large leaders of the Chapitos faction of the Sinaloa Cartel, both sons of convicted drug lord Joaquín 'El Chapo' Guzmán. The announcement was framed around severing the Chapitos' Chicago drug supply chain, which has been a primary focus of Northern District of Illinois federal prosecutors. Mochomito faces federal charges including drug trafficking conspiracy and racketeering. The doubling of his bounty signals an escalation in US pressure on the faction at a time when La Mayiza has pushed Chapitos fighters out of most of Sinaloa. Both brothers are believed to be sheltering in the Sierra Madre mountains in the Badiraguato region with diminished operational capacity.

media.caption: US doubles bounty on El Mochomito to $10M — $20M total for both Chapitos brothers

title: Joint Federal Operation Arrests 5 Chapitos Members on Escuinapa–Teacapán Highway, Seizes Arsenal
detail: A joint federal operation involving the Secretaría de Seguridad y Protección Ciudadana (SSPC), SEDENA, FGR, and the National Guard intercepted a vehicle on the Escuinapa–Teacapán highway in southern Sinaloa on April 16, 2026, detaining five alleged members of the Los Chapitos faction. The operation included aerial surveillance support. Seized assets included 7 long-barrel firearms, 1 pistol, ammunition magazines, cartridges, 6 ballistic vests, and 1 vehicle. The arrests came in the same municipality where 5 municipal police officers had been killed in the prior 16 days (March 31 ambush killing 4, April 8 home killing of 1 off-duty officer), and where 1,190 federal security personnel were deployed on April 10. The Escuinapa–Teacapán corridor is a key southern Sinaloa gateway and contested trafficking route toward Nayarit state. Combined with two Chapitos members arrested in Mazatlán on April 19, the week saw at least 7 Chapitos operatives detained by federal security forces.

media.caption: Five Chapitos members arrested on Escuinapa–Teacapán highway with 7 long-barrel firearms and 6 ballistic vests

title: García Harfuch Leads 10th Federal Security Cabinet in Culiacán — Announces C5 Expansion, Reports 2,000+ Detained
detail: Security Secretary Omar García Harfuch, Defense Secretary Ricardo Trevilla Trejo, and Sinaloa Governor Rubén Rocha Moya convened the 10th federal security cabinet visit to Sinaloa since October 2024 at the 9th Military Air Zone in Culiacán on April 16, 2026. Harfuch announced major milestones: more than 2,000 suspected criminals detained statewide, more than 2,000 clandestine drug laboratories destroyed, and expansion of the C5 (command, control, communications, computing, and intelligence) surveillance infrastructure into Culiacán and Mazatlán. New anti-kidnapping specialist units were also announced. Harfuch also met with local business leaders and academics to review the security strategy. The meeting took place on the same day that a joint federal operation arrested 5 Chapitos members in Escuinapa and the day after 6 violent deaths were recorded statewide — an indication of the continuing gap between macro security metrics and ground-level conditions. The repeated security cabinet visits reflect the federal government's political commitment to Sinaloa, its most complicated active security front.

media.caption: García Harfuch, Trevilla Trejo, and Governor Rocha Moya convene 10th federal security cabinet in Culiacán on April 16
